summarization.a: The multilayer plot shows the X-ray diffraction patterns of the as-deposited Ga₂O₃ thin film and the films annealed at 500 °C, 700 °C, and 900 °C temperatures. Crystallinity seems to improve and newer peaks started to reflect for higher annealing temperatures.

a.question: What is the objective behind annealing of the as-deposited Ga₂O₃ thin films at such higher temperatures?
a.answer: The main objective of annealing the as-deposited Ga₂O₃ thin films at high temperatures is to improve their crystallinity, remove defects, and transform the film from an amorphous or poorly ordered state into a well-crystallized phase with better structural and electrical properties.

a.question: What changes in peak intensity were observed with rising annealing temperatures when compared to the as-deposited film and the films annealed at different temperatures?
a.answer:
The as-deposited and 500 °C–annealed Ga₂O₃ films were amorphous, and except for the strong Si peak, no Ga₂O₃ diffraction peaks were detected.
When the annealing temperature was increased to 700 °C, the film transformed into β phase-Ga₂O₃ with a preferred (400) orientation. The intensity of this (400) peak increased further with higher annealing temperatures.
At 900 °C, the film showed additional orientations, including (002) and/or (–202), indicating the development of more crystalline planes at higher temperatures.

a.question: Did the as-deposited films show any structural changes after annealing at higher temperatures?
a.answer: Yes. The films changed from amorphous to crystalline β-Ga₂O₃ with distinct orientations as the annealing temperature increased.

a.question: Does annealing improve the performance of the films?
a.answer:
Yes.
Annealing generally improves the performance of Ga₂O₃ thin films. By increasing the annealing temperature, the films transform from an amorphous state to a well-crystallized β-Ga₂O₃ phase, develop stronger preferred orientations, and reduce structural defects. These improvements usually enhance electrical, optical, and structural properties, leading to better overall film performance.
